summary: Gruber continues his critique of TheStreet.com columnist Scott Moritz for pushing a fabricated narrative that Apple expected to sell one million iPhones during its opening weekend. Moritz reported a rumor about Apple cutting iPhone production orders, citing a note from Miller Tabak that turned out not to exist. Gruber dismantles the 'one million iPhones' claim by pointing out that Apple sold 270,000 units on the first night and weekend, and stores were nearly sold out by Tuesday — making it logically impossible that Apple expected to sell four times more. He argues that a segment of the business press is determined to frame the iPhone launch as a disappointment despite clear evidence to the contrary.
key_points: Moritz cited a Miller Tabak note about iPhone production cuts that the firm itself said doesn't exist
The 'whisper number' claim that Apple expected to sell one million iPhones opening weekend is mathematically impossible given supply constraints
Apple sold 270,000 iPhones Friday night and Saturday, with most stores sold out by Tuesday — they couldn't have expected 700,000 more sales with no inventory
Gruber identifies a pattern in business press coverage attempting to reframe the iPhone's strong debut as a disappointment
money_quotes: What Moritz published on TheStreet.com was false.
You can argue whether the iPhone's debut was great or really great, but you can't argue that Apple had actually hoped to sell more than twice as many of them as they actually did.
Clearly, though, there's a contingent of the business press attempting to argue just that.
How could Apple possibly have expected or even hoped to sell 700,000 additional iPhones over the opening weekend if they were nearly sold out of them by Tuesday?
Bad news seems even less likely to leak than good news, and Apple rarely leaks these days.
key_insight: A segment of the financial press was manufacturing a narrative of iPhone disappointment by citing fabricated sources and making claims that were mathematically impossible given the actual supply and sales data.
